name: Payroll
short_description: You owe the loan shark everything. Chain devastating combos, break the game wide open, and outsmart the odds. A roguelike deckbuilder where clever beats lucky. Pray it's enough.
detailed_description:
CONTROL THE CHAOS This isn't a luck simulator. This is strategic dice manipulation. Throw 3 dice. Rotate each die to any visible face. Score to pay your debts.

BUILD YOUR PERFECT DICE After each roll, spend pips in the shop to upgrade your dice. Add new faces, craft synergies, and build something that probably shouldn't work — until it does. Every decision compounds. Every run feels different.

FACE THE LOAN SHARK Clear your debts and the shark himself shows up  and he doesn't play fair.

Boss rounds twist the rules with random modifiers

8 DICE SETS TO UNLOCK Each die isn't just a reskin. Different starting faces, unique passive abilities, and entirely new combo potential mean every unlocked die opens a fresh tactical rabbit hole.

 Over 100 Unique Dice Faces – Massive replay value with wildly different playstyles

 Rotate Dice to Control Your Luck – Skill-based strategy meets tactical decision-making

 7 Unlockable Dice – Each die transforms how you play

 Boss Rounds with Random Modifiers – Endless tactical challenges that keep you on your toes

 Endless Mode – Push your limits as difficulty scales infinitely

 15-30 Minute Runs – Perfect bite-sized roguelike sessions

 True Roguelike – No permanent upgrades, only pure skill progression

 Strategic Combo Crafting – Discover and build devastating synergies

 High Replayability – Every run feels fresh and unpredictable

NO SAFETY NETS. NO EXCUSES. No meta-progression. No permanent upgrades. Every run starts from zero and ends in either triumph or debt.

Build from blank dice to devastating combos in 10 rounds. Think you've figured it out? Endless Mode is waiting.

FOR FANS OF:

Strategic dice manipulation, roguelike deckbuilding, and tactical combo crafting. If you love controlling chaos and discovering powerful synergies, Payroll delivers skill-based strategy wrapped in addictive roguelike replayability.

Simple to learn. Impossible to master.

Throw. Rotate. Score. Shop. Survive.

Can you pay off the loan shark before he cashes in?

reviews.review_text:
[h1]SUPER EDIT[/h1]
Within LESS THAN 24 HOURS of my review, the developer not only responded, but pushed a fix FULLY fixing my only disappointment with the game. My only complaint is 100% gone now, and given such a responsive and proactive dev, I see a very bright future for this game and would bump up the price point at which I'd recommend it. I'm leaving most of my review intact, but striking out the singular complaint that's no longer relevant.

[h3]Original Review:[/h3]
I first played Payroll during a recent Next Fest, not sure if it was the last or if before then, so the 1.8 hour count is misleading and is closer to 6 hours. In fact, most of the demos I tried to play 30-60 minutes of and no more so that I could get through a dozen or so, but by the time I realized I had played at least 30min of Payroll, it was 4 hours later.

The core loop is extremely enjoyable for Payroll; customizing your dice and creating synergies is extremely fun. I'm not super good at games in general, nor do I often venture into high difficulty modes, but at least base difficulty, I've found the balance to be a good mix of challenging yet not frustratingly punishing.

[strike]My only complaint, if you can call it that, is really more of a mixed bag. At the start of a run, you're given random faces on your dice from a large pool of possibilities -- and that can basically make or break the beginning of the game. I've had some runs where I struggled to even beat the first table (and eventually started just checking the dice an abandoning any 'trash' runs), and others where the faces I get are so good that I finish the first bet in one roll. When I say that this game is a roll of the dice, that's not maybe how I wanted to mean it. I do think many people will view this as a positive though, as it keeps each run feeling fresh, but I can't help but feel like there's a better way to do it somehow. Maybe instead runs could start you with a certain number of pips and you get 1-3 rounds of shops to pick from? Idk what the best solution is, but it feels like there's really high variance that's completely out of the player's control at the start.[/strike]
[b]As mentioned in the edit, this has been completely fixed![/b]

In general, I definitely recommend Payroll. I'm not sure if I'd fully recommend it at $13 personally, even if I bought it for 90% of that on launch, but if you're not hurting for cash, it's definitely a fun game. And if you ever see it on sale for $8-10, then I think absolutely anyone with any passing interest in chance-based roguelikes should pick it up for that much. [b]Update:[/b] I bumped it up to $8-10 given the dev's proactive responsiveness, bc I appreciate that a lot in a dev. For the sake of recording my original words, the original review said $5-8.
Excellent game, really tests your strategy. Recommend to anyone who wants a challenge

An excellent way to pass the time with a simple goal in mind which is to make as much coin as you can!

The items known as 'faces' varies mechanically in such a rich way, giving the players both an exploration factor as to what they might find whilst really making you think about optimal strategies in mind.

I believe there is something here for everyone from the gameplay at its core being a roguelike which has become so prevalent with newer games today, bragging rights to be made when shooting for that spot at the top of the leader boards giving that competitive edge and for the completionist's who will want to obtain everything at hand via the collections tab.
Kept up with the development of the game, its pretty fun to relax and chill after a busy day :D Highly recommend.
